issue_title: bug(router): remote etcd hosts not properly configurable
issue_body:
`deis-router` sources its _own_ address _and_ the address of a machine running etcd from the _same_ environment variable:

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/router/boot.go#L39-L49

```
host := getopt("HOST", "127.0.0.1")

// ...

hostEtcdPath := getopt("HOST_ETCD_PATH", "/deis/router/hosts/"+host)

// ...

client := etcd.NewClient([]string{"http://" + host + ":" + etcdPort})
```

This creates the unfortunate limitation of not being able to run the `deis-router` on any machine that isn't also running etcd.

According to CoreOS' own cluster architecture recommendations, it's not advisable to run etcd on more than nine nodes.  This means that large clusters, should run etcd on a small (probably fixed) number of nodes that run "common services," with a more elastic pool of worker nodes reserved for everything else.  In the case of a large Deis cluster, most Deis components probably run along with the "common services" as well.  Notable exceptions include `deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher`, which must run on the worker nodes.  (`deis-logspout` actually needs to run on both.)  I would argue that in a _large_ cluster where the `deis-router` component may need to scale to many instances, an admin might reasonably elect to schedule and scale that component on worker nodes.  But if the worker nodes aren't running etcd (and we've established they would not in such a case) then the code above is problematic.

Reference: https://coreos.com/docs/cluster-management/setup/cluster-architectures/

`deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher` both seem to address this issue by use of a dedicated `ETCD_HOST` environment variable.  The same approach could be used here, although I would argue that all three would benefit from having a comma delimited set of `ETCD_PEERS` specified instead, which can be parsed to an array and passed to `etcd.NewClient(...)` as that would improve their overall resilience.  Today, there would be problems using the host specified by `ETCD_HOST`if that host went offline-- even if the etcd cluster as a whole managed to maintain quorum/health.

issue_title: Discussion: improving the provisioning process
issue_body:
The purpose of this issue is to highlight some pain points or general problems with the existing provisioning processes in order to fuel discussion directed at improving those processes.

Disclaimer: I am most familiar with the AWS provisioning process, so my comments are most informed by that.  If others want to call out other pain points or general problems with the process for other providers, go right ahead!

discovery URLs
---------------------

Generating user-data: many operators are well-acquainted with using `make discovery-url` to generate user-data.  What that make task does not do-- probably by design-- is it doesn't delete  / overwrite existing user-data.  The adverse affect of this is someone who has already failed in a prior attempt to provision a Deis cluster or is provisioning a second one might inadvertently re-use an etcd discovery URL without realizing they are doing so (even though they have re-run `make discovery-url`).

User-data and templates
---------------------------------

Apart from provisioning scripts, we also provide "update" scripts that allow a CloudFormation template (for AWS, e.g.) to be re-run.  If an operator were to use this and in the time since provisioning a cluster, his or her user-data has changed (e.g. perhaps they altered it to stand up a second cluster) _or_ any environment variables that have an impact on the composition of the generated template or the parameters passed to it have changed, then the result is all of these undesired changes might accidentally be applied to an existing cluster.  This isn't ideal and could in fact cause some pretty big problems.

We should probably save user-data, templates, and parameters used in the provisioning process so that they can be tweaked by an advanced operator and / or re-used by an update process _as is_ (without the need to regenerate those artifacts).  This would allow an operator to keep the artifacts that accurately describe his or her clusters in source control.

Environment variables
------------------------------

The provisioning process is highly dependent on environment variables set by the operator to specify a variety of options or other required inputs.  As noted in the previous section, if these environment variables change between the time of initial provisioning and an update, some bad things can happen, but beyond that, relying on these environment variables makes for a clunky and error-prone user experience.  It is all too easy, for instance, to get all the way through the provisioning process never having set the `DEIS_NUM_INSTANCES` environment variable.  As a result, one ends up with an etcd cluster size of three by default.  What is five were desired, as is often the case in production?  At such a juncture, the only choice is to start over.

Virtual network topology
--------------------------------

Currently, by default (at least in AWS) we provision a VPC for each new Deis cluster.  We do offer the option of deploying a Deis cluster into an existing VPC instead.  The problems here are threefold:

* The VPCs we create only utilize two availability zones.  Quorum is more easily guaranteed in the face of inevitable network problems if the nodes of an etcd cluster are distributed across _at least_ three zones.

* As mentioned, we offer the possibility of provisioning a Deis cluster into an existing VPC, and when one does so, we also offer operators the possibility of deploying their cluster into private subnets.  IMO, this is actually a requirement for a production-worthy cluster.  The VPCs we create do not include private subnets.  IMO, this should at least be an option.

* In the default case where an operator elects to let our provisioning process create the VPC for their cluster, we generate a _single_ CloudFormation template that defines _both_ the VPC and the Deis cluster.  This isn't ideal for a number of reasons.  For one, it means we are more rapidly approaching the limit on CF template size.  Second, it means the existence of the VPC is atomically bound to the existence of the Deis cluster.  One cannot choose to delete the Deis cluster without also deleting the VPC-- which could be problematic if additional Deis clusters have been deployed into that VPC afterwards.  Third, in many organizations, network security gurus who most likely do not have and do not require extensive knowledge of Deis are going to want to tweak certain aspects of a VPC, such as route tables and network ACLs.  For all of these reasons, it is probably best that any option we provider for establishing a VPC is _decoupled_ from the creation of Deis clusters.

Isolating etcd and the control plane
----------------------------------------------

I think this issue is well understood, so I will skip over the details, but we _do_ recommend that in production, operators isolate both etcd and the Deis control plane to a small, fixed number of nodes, while allowing their data plane to scale more extensively.  In AWS terms, this requires _two_ sets of user-data (which vary just slightly from one another) to be used in the launch configuration for _two_ separate auto-scaling groups.  Although we highly recommend this, we haven't done more than document the recommendation with some modest advice on how one can achieve this themselves.  Our provisioning processes simply don't account for these recommendations, although they probably should.

Lowering the barriers for provisioning
-------------------------------------------------

Provisioning, again with respect to AWS, currently requires the AWS CLI to have been installed, as well as Python.  It seems there may be a growing consensus that Docker, instead, should be the lowest common denominator in terms of tooling that you assume (or hope) every operator has installed.  Containerizing the provisioning process would probably make life easier for most operators.

issue_title: Allow two-step (i.e. retry-safe) task submission
issue_body:
`globus transfer async-transfer` and `globus transfer async-delete` currently hide away the submission ID generation. That means that the CLI doesn't support reliable, safe-to-retry task submission.

To resolve, we should expose this behavior with a new command and a couple of flags.

Namely, we want `globus transfer task generate-submission-id` to produce the submission ID in raw text, and `globus transfer async-transfer --submission-id` to consume it.
The same option should exist for `async-delete`, of course.

issue_title: StateDownloader should retry immediately after receiving empty reply from peer
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

During a state sync, sometimes a peer won't have the trie nodes we requested, and so will send us an empty `NodeData` msg.  When that happens we just move on and those nodes are re-requested only after the timeout (20s) has passed.

### How can it be fixed

Whenever we get an empty `NodeData` from a peer, we should look up the nodes that were originally requested to that peer and re-request them to a different peer.

issue_title: Explore alternative for current configuration approach
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The way I understand it, we currently use `Configurable` as a way to dynamically generate classes (or maybe better: class like objects?). We use that all over the place e.g.

```python
FrontierVM = VM.configure(
    # class name
    __name__='FrontierVM',
    # fork name
    fork='frontier',
    # classes
    _state_class=FrontierState,
    # helpers
    create_header_from_parent=staticmethod(create_frontier_header_from_parent),
    compute_difficulty=staticmethod(compute_frontier_difficulty),
    configure_header=configure_frontier_header,
)
```

The way I understand the mechanism, it basically lets you take a class as a blueprint and generate a new one with several properties and sub properties altered.

I'm not entirely sure if all of its functionality could be achieved with regular style inheritance or if it contains things that would not work with classical inheritance. In other words, I'm not sure if it is just syntactic sugar to have a more concise mechanism or if contains stuff that would be hard to achieve the classical way. I'll assume that **it does** have some special power for now.

While there is no big problem with this approach per se I noticed a couple of minor things that I considered worth enough to write down.

- the current mechanism doesn't play nice with `mypy` or types in general. In the above example `FrontierVM` really acts as a class but isn't recognized as such from the type system

- as an extension to the previous point, the type system doesn't know that `FrontierVM` inherits from `VM`

- not having proper `class` syntax makes it harder to document as one can not simple write e.g.

```python

class FrontierVM
    """
    Frontier anyone?
    """
    ...
```

- just from looking at the code it is less obvious that this thing really is meant to be treated as a class

### How can it be fixed

1. If we assume that there is nothing `Configurable` achieves that the classical inheritance style could not achieve than obviously we could simply *consider* using the classical style if we think it would be worthwhile.

2. Alternatively, I guess we could come up with some kind of decorator based approach that might translate the example above into something roughly equivalent to the following.

```python

@configure(
    # fork name
    fork='frontier',
    # classes
    _state_class=FrontierState,
    # helpers
    create_header_from_parent=staticmethod(create_frontier_header_from_parent),
    compute_difficulty=staticmethod(compute_frontier_difficulty),
    configure_header=configure_frontier_header,
)
class FrontierVM(VM):
    """
    Definitely Frontier! 
    """
    pass
```

This isn't a real proposal for something I think we *should do*. I don't know much about the reasoning behind the current mechanism so I'm just putting this out as something to discuss.

```

HTTPS_PROXY=http://myproxy:8080
```

``conda=4.0.8`` successfully picks up my ``%HTTPS_PROXY%`` and installs ``conda=4.1``:

```
$ conda install conda=4.1
Fetching package metadata: ..........
Solving package specifications: .........

The following packages will be UPDATED:

```

now trying to revert back to ``conda=4.0``:

Fetching package metadata .........
Could not connect to https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/
Connection error: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='repo.continuum.io', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url...

proxy settings no longer work. Configuring ``proxy_servers`` in ``.condarc`` restores connectivity.

issue_title: Possible regression when setting `envs_dirs`
issue_body:
From the conda [docs](https://conda.io/docs/install/central.html) for `envs_dir` option in a condarc file:
> Specify directories in which environments are located. If this key is set, the root prefix envs_dir is not used unless explicitly included. This key also determines where the package caches will be located.

In conda 4.0.5 and 4.1.11 this works as expected and, when the `envs_dirs` optionis specified, conda only uses the specified location for environments/cacheing packages.

In conda 4.2.12 through 4.3.9 the root env-dir/package-cache is still available (listed second) which means conda will try to link packages cahced in the the root environment.

In conda 4.3.11 the package cache is missing from the configured `envs_dir` and it look like conda will default to the users home folder.

I'm aware that conda is moving towards multi-user support and I'm not sure if these changes are bugs or part of the new intended behaviour.
If this is an intended change, will there be an option to strictly configure which package cache(s) are used (i.e. avoid the use of some caches)?

Possibly related to:
https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1329
https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3885

**Details:**

Tested in Ubuntu 16.04 and equivalent results found in a centralised installation on CentOS 7.

condarc file:

```
envs_dirs:
  -  /home/tim/Environments

issue_title: Command line option to suppress start menu icon installation in windows

**I'm submitting a...**

  - [x] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

I would like to propose a command line option for the Windows Miniconda installer that avoids the `menuinst` step. This is to enable non-user-visible installations, whose icons otherwise clobber the "main" miniconda start menu shortcut. 

In principle, it would be consistent with the spirit of the existing /NoRegistry . (In fact, not installing the icon should be default in this case, in my opinion).

issue_title: hardcoded 'python' and 'lua' behave like landmines for custom package/environment setups
issue_body:
Was setting up some metapackages to simplify our conda build configurations, and ran into this mysterious error:

```
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: Removing old build environment
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: BUILD START: sequoiacore-1.0-wilypy35_0
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: Fetching package metadata: ....
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: Solving package specifications: .
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: WARNING: Could not find any versions of conda-build in the channels
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT: Error:  Package missing in current linux-64 channels: 
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: STDOUT:   - lua None*
2016-05-29 06:21:19:  0: INFO: Process exit code: 1
```

This appears to be caused by the line

https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/f705ab14600cfe02a0335814cffcd6dd96a717d6/conda/plan.py#L348

which has some hardcoded stuff for 'python' and 'lua'. I can obviously get around this in many ways, but this kind of thing provides annoying landmines for custom setups isolated from the mainline default packages.

issue_title: Conda checks for newer versions in channels in current context instead of only base channels
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior My base environment uses only the default channels. When I attempted to create a new environment using the conda-forge channel, conda found a newer conda version in conda-forge and warned about it. However, there is no newer version in the default channels to install. ### Steps to Reproduce ``` (base) λ conda create --name test --channel conda-forge --dry-run Solving environment: done ==> WARNING: A newer version of conda exists. <== current version: 4.5.0 latest version: 4.5.1 Please update conda by running $ conda update -n base conda ## Package Plan ## environment location: C:\Users\stan.west\Programs\Miniconda3-64\envs\test (base) λ conda update conda Solving environment: done # All requested packages already installed. ``` At the moment, conda-forge has conda-4.5.1, but the default channels have only conda-4.5.0: ``` (base) λ conda search --channel conda-forge "conda>=4.5" Loading channels: done # Name Version Build Channel conda 4.5.0 py27_0 conda-forge conda 4.5.0 py27_0 pkgs/main conda 4.5.0 py35_0 conda-forge conda 4.5.0 py35_0 pkgs/main conda 4.5.0 py36_0 conda-forge conda 4.5.0 py36_0 pkgs/main conda 4.5.1 py27_0 conda-forge conda 4.5.1 py35_0 conda-forge conda 4.5.1 py36_0 conda-forge ``` ## Expected Behavior Conda seeks newer versions only in channels used in the base environment and ignores newer versions in other channels. ## Environment Information

issue_title: `get_cigar_stats` documentation is inaccurate since support for B-tag was added
issue_body:
The `AlignedSegment.get_cigar_stats` method returns arrays of length 11, not length 10 as implied by the docstring.

The discrepancy is due to the addition of support for a "B" cigar tag that has not been reflected in the docstring (see e6c9bda, although the release notes suggest that the B tag has been recognised since pysam version 0.7).

The `AlignedSegment.get_cigar_stats` is as follows:

```
        """summary of operations in cigar string.
        The output order in the array is "MIDNSHP=X" followed by a
        field for the NM tag. If the NM tag is not present, this
        field will always be 0.
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |M    |BAM_CMATCH    |0    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |I    |BAM_CINS      |1    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |D    |BAM_CDEL      |2    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |N    |BAM_CREF_SKIP |3    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |S    |BAM_CSOFT_CLIP|4    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |H    |BAM_CHARD_CLIP|5    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |P    |BAM_CPAD      |6    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |=    |BAM_CEQUAL    |7    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |X    |BAM_CDIFF     |8    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |NM   |NM tag        |9    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        If no cigar string is present, empty arrays will be returned.
        Parameters
        ----------
        Returns
        -------
        arrays : two arrays. The first contains the nucleotide counts within
           each cigar operation, the second contains the number of blocks for
           each cigar operation.
        """
```

I propose this be ammended to something like:

```
        """summary of operations in cigar string.
        The output order in the array is "MIDNSHP=XB" followed by a
        field for the NM tag. If the NM tag is not present, this
        field will always be 0.
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |M    |BAM_CMATCH    |0    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |I    |BAM_CINS      |1    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |D    |BAM_CDEL      |2    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |N    |BAM_CREF_SKIP |3    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |S    |BAM_CSOFT_CLIP|4    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |H    |BAM_CHARD_CLIP|5    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |P    |BAM_CPAD      |6    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |=    |BAM_CEQUAL    |7    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |X    |BAM_CDIFF     |8    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |B    |B tag         |9    |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        |NM   |NM tag        |10   |
        +-----+--------------+-----+
        If no cigar string is present, empty arrays will be returned.
        Parameters
        ----------
        Returns
        -------
        arrays : two arrays. The first contains the nucleotide counts within
           each cigar operation, the second contains the number of blocks for
           each cigar operation.
        """
```

The B tag is not standard SAM and I haven't been able to find a definition: can anyone shed light on what it means? Someone who knows what this B tag is about might suggest a better entry for the table.

issue_title: Possible query alignment length bug?
issue_body:
It's very possible that I've missed something strange about this alignment, but at first glance this appears to be a bug because since query_alignment_length is supposed to be qend - qstart (according to the [documentation](http://pysam.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignedSegment.query_alignment_length)), and it's not:

    >>> import pysam
    >>> bf = pysam.AlignmentFile('example.sam')
    >>> read = bf.next()
    >>> read.query_length
    51
    >>> read.query_alignment_length
    51
    >>> read.query_alignment_start
    0
    >>> read.query_alignment_end
    35
    >>> len(read.query_alignment_sequence)
    35

`example.sam` can be retrieved for reproducibility from: https://gist.githubusercontent.com/vsbuffalo/94be7da4654d2af37c06/raw/c28fd61b34cd1ab811a8b3c1580c70887e685d1a/example.sam (it's a single read from 1KG data).

    >>> pysam.__version__
    '0.8.1'

Installed via pip.

issue_title: Add support for EAPI autoComplete feature

issue_title: create_index filters support
issue_body:
Hello,

We have weekly indexes. For e.g. node_2016_31, node_2016_32 etc.

We use the curator `alias` action, with the correct filters in the add/remove sections to create an alias for last_3_months (comprising of last 12 weekly indexes) and this works.

We would like to also create the index for next week, this week itself so that the alias will work smoothly. The `create_index` action does not support `filters` and `filtertype` like [alias command](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/examples/actions/alias.yml).

Such a support would be very helpful. Or is there any other way to do this.

issue_title: Increase default client timeout for remote reindex
issue_body: When reading indices from remote cluster, if it takes longer than 10 seconds, you could experience a timeout.  This may not be common, but it could result in a bad experience.  Bump this to 30 seconds, or even 3 minutes.

issue_title: latest aiohttp support for 5.x branch
issue_body:
Issue #37 was fixed for elasticsearch-py-async versions 6.x and above.
Version 6.0.0 depends on elasticsearch 6.0.0, with has compatability with ElasticSearch 6.x

In real world, Elasticsearch 5.x is widely installed, which means elasticsearch-py-async 5.x is needed to talk to ES 5.x cluster.

Using 5.x causes the same issue as #37 with latest aiohttp.

Is there a way to get the `Timeout` to `async_timeout` change implemented for 5.x branch?

issue_title: [Keyvault][2.0.6] "az keyvault secret list" doesn't show "name" of secret, but download/show require it
issue_body:
### Description
To use keyvault as a rsa keystore, I'll need to use **download** and **show** when I setting up new local machines (let alone servers in Azure). But both of those require `--name` of the secret. Unfortunately, the `name` value is not displayed by the output of `list`. 

Result: I can't script directly from `list`, because I have to put a `sed` from `xargs` in there to calculate the name from the `id` value that _is_ returned.

GOAL: the metadata from `list` has the `name` value already in it so that you can easily script the keys from there without interpolating `xargs/sed`.

EXAMPLE:

```
az keyvault secret list --vault-name xxx

issue_title: List subscriptions with user's role
issue_body:
We are using azure-cli for our automation scripts, and we would like to get a list of all subscriptions that user is an owner of (or an administrator).
 
We couldn't find any easy way to do it, so we would do it as follow:

1. `az account list` and retrieved an email from `user.name`

2. Retrieve user's objectId by `az ad user list --query "[?mail=='<EMAIL>']"`

3. And finally execute `az role assignment list --query "[?principalId=='<OBJECT_ID>']"` to get a role.

The main issue is that in some cases in the 2. step, not every user has a mail assigned to it (why?).
Also, all these steps are a bit cumbersome, especially if a user has lots of subscriptions. I hope there is a better way.

To summarise, is there a way to retrieve current user's objectId or list all subscriptions with user's role(s) in it?

issue_title: [DNS] How do I add tags with a space in the value?
issue_body:
'az network dns zone create -h' shows:

    --tags                        : Space separated tags in 'key[=value]' format. Use "" to clear
                                    existing tags.

Tags cannot contain a space in the key. However, they can contain a space in the value.  (I just tried it via the Portal, it works).

Given that the list of tags is space-separated, how does this work? How do I create a tag with a space in the value?

If it's not supported, then it needs to be.  If it is supported, then the help text needs to be improved.

I found this in the context of creating a DNS zone.  However, I expect it is widespread, and hence the fix should be applied broadly across the CLI surface.

issue_title: Improve azure-keyvault custom Authentication mecanism
issue_body:
`requests` has nice ability to accept a subclass of AuthBase to custom the authentication process. This is callback-based, and `requests` gives to the `__call__` method a prepared query. Note that when you do `requests.get` or any methods, requests creates for you a prepared query first, and this is this query that is sent to `AuthBase` subclasses.
However, not all possible parameters are inside a prepared query. For example, `verify`, that you can specify in your call (ie: `requests.get(...., verify='myfile')`) is not used in the prepared query. In addition, we don't have access to the context (i.e. are we using a Session?).

KeyVault has a special behavior that requires to do a requests from AuthBase to get the token. With the preceding limitation, this implies that we don't have access to the following parameters from within an AuthBase: proxies, stream, verify, cert, timeout, alow_redirects. We don't have access too to the configuration of the Session that is doing the call. To end, AuthBase is called before environment variable like REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE or CURL_CA_BUNDLE are read.

The probable best approach is to provide auth dynamically:
```python
client.certificates.get(......., auth=KeyAuthBase(.....))
```
This way we can create this class with the context and drop it after the call. Another way is to add support for that in msrest. Since msrest owns the Session and the `requests` call, we are in the right place to fix the context.

FYI @schaabs

issue_title: Help to developted azure python sdk.
azure.servicebus.ServiceBusService should support initialization from connection string
issue_body:
The C# Azure SDK supports CreateFromConnectionString() method that takes a SBS connection string. See https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-dotnet-get-started-with-queues

Python SDK however does not seem to support this, and user is forced to parse the connection string into service_namespace, shared_access_key_name, shared_access_key_value parameters. See https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/service-bus-messaging/service-bus-python-how-to-use-queues

This is a request for python SDK to support ServiceBusService initialization from connection string.

issue_title: Incorporate cache time when enqueuing a set
issue_body: `ci.find_batch_sources()` sorts by reverse time, but currently only pays attention to `process time`, which is misleading for slow-to-cache ESRI sources. `ci.get_batch_run_times()` returns strings, but should instead parse `cache time` and `process time` and add them together, for a truer picture of actual time. issue_title: Sub app middlewares are called before main app,
issue_body:
## Long story short

According to http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web.html#nested-applications,
`[I]f URL is '/admin/something' middlewares from [main application] are applied first and [sub application] middlewares are the next in the call chain.` 

It turns out it's actually the other way.

## Steps to reproduce

Run this code saved as foo.py:
```python
#!/usr/bin/python3 -tt

import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web

async def middleware1(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware1")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler
    
async def middleware2(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware2")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler

async def middleware3(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware3")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler

app = aiohttp.web.Application(
    middlewares=[middleware1])

subapp1 = aiohttp.web.Application(middlewares=[middleware2])
app.add_subapp("/foo", subapp1)

subapp2 = aiohttp.web.Application(middlewares=[middleware3])
subapp1.add_subapp("/foo/bar", subapp2)

aiohttp.web.run_app(app, host='127.0.0.1', port=4096)
```

And the run `wget http://127.0.0.1:4096/foo/bar`.

### Current Result:
```
$ python3 foo.py
======== Running on http://127.0.0.1:4096 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
middleware3
middleware2
middleware1
```

### Expected output:
```
$ python3 foo.py
======== Running on http://127.0.0.1:4096 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
middleware1
middleware2
middleware3
```

## Your environment

Ubuntu Xenial (python 3.5.2) with aiohttp 2.0.7

issue_title: Degrading performance over time...
issue_body:
I wrote a quick AsyncScraper class below:

	import logging, datetime
	import aiohttp
	import asyncio
	import uvloop

	# asyncio.set_event_loop_policy(uvloop.EventLoopPolicy())

	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
	logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
	logging.basicConfig(format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')
	logger.addHandler(logging.StreamHandler())

	class AsyncScraper(object):
		headers = {"User-Agent" : 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_9_2) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/34.0.1847.131 Safari/537.36'}
		def __init__(self, number_workers=100, max_connections=1000, timeout=10):
			self.number_workers = number_workers
			self.max_connections = max_connections
			self.timeout = timeout
			self.work_queue = asyncio.Queue()
			self.cookie_jar = aiohttp.CookieJar(unsafe=True)
			self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=self.max_connections, verify_ssl=False), cookie_jar=self.cookie_jar)

		def __del__(self):
			self.session.close()

		async def get_response(self, url):
			try:
				with aiohttp.Timeout(self.timeout):
					async with self.session.get(url, allow_redirects=True, headers=AsyncScraper.headers) as response:
						content = await response.text()
						return {'error': "", 'status': response.status, 'url':url, 'content': content, 'timestamp': str(datetime.datetime.utcnow())}
			except Exception as err:
				return {'error': err, 'status': "", 'url':url, 'content': "", 'timestamp': str(datetime.datetime.utcnow())}

		async def consumer(self, worker_id):
			results = []
			while not self.work_queue.empty():
				queue_url = await self.work_queue.get()

				logger.debug("processing {} from worker_{}".format(queue_url, worker_id))
				response = await self.get_response(queue_url)
				logger.debug("received {} from worker_{}".format(queue_url, worker_id))
				if response['error'] is "":
					results.append(response)
			logger.debug("finished processing {} urls via worker_{}".format(len(results), worker_id))
			return results

		def get_all(self, urls):
			[self.work_queue.put_nowait(url) for url in urls]
			tasks = asyncio.gather(*[self.consumer(worker_id) for worker_id in range(self.number_workers)], return_exceptions=True)
			loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
			results = loop.run_until_complete(tasks)
			return [__ for _ in results for __ in _]

issue_title: aiohttp 2.x closing client request data stream
issue_body:
In previous versions of aiohttp, if you passed a stream as the `data` parameter to an aiohttp request, it would not close the stream after the request, now it is closing it because it's wrapping it in a `Payload`, and later the `write` method closes it.

I believe aiohttp should not be closing the stream sent to it. This new behavior caused this issue with aiobotocore: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/issues/221 resulting in retries failing.

issue_title: Proposal for event handling refactoring..
issue_body:
As of 0.9.1.0 we do support various combinations for adding event handlers, some of them seem overdone and unnecessary. They add to a code complexity and don't bring anything extra to the feature-set. Below I discuss what I think are the required feature-set for EventHandling. Asking the community for comments.

* Watch methods
    * watch an Mo for any change. Provide a callback function that will be called anytime any property of a specified Mo changes.
    * watch an Mo for a specific condition `mo.prop == value`; where value can also be a single value or a list of values. This is a derivative of the above and ideally can be done at the application layer by the user, but this seems to be the most common case a usage scenario and so we should support this in the base sdk.

* Other considerations:
    * We also need to support both the `push` and `poll` mode for the above combinations.
      * Push mode - SDK continuously listens to the events from the server and acts when the correct event is found
      * Poll mode - SDK polls the servers regularly for the required Mo and checks the condition

* Wait method"
    * Additionally, we should also have a wait method that can keep the script alive for the duration of the eventHandler.

issue_title: Enhancement: allow for custom sort order of kernel specs
issue_body:
Currently, kernel specs are sorted by their display name. The sorting logic is encapsulated in [kernelselector.js](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/notebook/js/kernelselector.js) and tricky to customize. Provide some way to allow for a custom sort order. Example use cases:
1. In the [Data Science Experience](url), notebooks for Apache Spark services can choose from several programming languages and combine these with different Spark versions. We want the higher Spark versions listed before the lower ones.  
_Disclaimer:_ I'm a developer working on the Data Sciene Experience.
2. A post on the Jupyter mailing list asked for "favorite kernels" being listed first, with the option of hiding other kernels in a submenu. The list of favorite kernels is defined by the operator of the notebook server, not by the users.  
https://groups.google.com/forum/?utm_source=digest&utm_medium=email#!topic/jupyter/qhDpFTuxzgs

Implementation options could be to customize the sorting logic, or to add metadata to kernel specs. We observed that with notebook 5.0, the kernel specs are sometimes loaded before `custom.js`, so replacing the sorting logic at that point is a problem. Also, there are two different implementations of the sorting logic in `kernelselector.js`.
Adding an optional "sort key" attribute to kernel specs, which defaults to the display name, would allow hosting services to customize the sort order of the provided kernels. In cases where the notebook server is backend by a kernel gateway, the sort order would be under control of the kernel gateway operator.

issue_title: Display data as a cell type
issue_body:
On the heels of https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/pull/621 and https://github.com/IRkernel/IRkernel/issues/260, I'm wondering about a cell that matches display data semantics. We already have a way to display `mimetype: data` bundles.

Here's an example UX flow. A user goes to insert an image either via drag and drop or a menu:

```
Insert
    --> Image
    --> HTML
```

The image then ends up embedded in the document as if it was injected using magics or running code.

Thinking on @lbustelo's use case for the declarative widgets, this would allow cross-language support for writing direct HTML cells without the use of magics. The cell then has two states - edit and view, just like the markdown cells.

This also spares the idea of having to do garbage collection in #621.

/cc @lbustelo @parente @julienr @jdfreder

issue_title: Implement software_agent token generation
issue_body:
The software_agent functionality is live at https://dev.dataservice.duke.edu/apiexplorer.
What this boils down to is there is a new endpoint ```POST /api/v1/software_agents/api_token``` that will return a auth token for use with all the other endpoints. This endpoint doesn't require authentication.
It requires two keys:
- User API key: ```GET /api/v1/current_user/api_key```
- Software Agent API key: ```GET /api/v1/software_agents/{id}/api_key``` where id is the uuid for our software agent.

**The troublesome part is the token is only good for two hours.** So a really long download or upload will fail with a 401 after that time.  We could try to get this extended, but given a big enough file we will run into this issue.

issue_title: [numpy 1.8.x] Array slices broken?
issue_body:
See the following test case:

```
import autograd.numpy as np
import autograd

def fn1(x):
    a = np.array([x[0], x[1]])
    b = np.array([x[2], x[3]])
    return np.dot(a, b)

f = autograd.value_and_grad(fn1)
print f(np.ones(4))
```
produces `(2.0, array([ 1.,  1.,  1.,  1.]))`, naturally.

The identical code using array slices for `a` and `b`:
```
def fn2(x):
    a = x[0:2]
    b = x[2:4]
    return np.dot(a, b)

f = autograd.value_and_grad(fn2)
print f(np.ones(4))
```
throws an exception in `autograd/numpy/numpy_extra.pyc` on line 123 in `primitive_sum_arrays(*arrays)`, trying to call `np.add.at`. I haven't figured out which argument is the slice here.

issue_title: Reading unexpected attribute type crashes
issue_body:
I encountered this when I tried to read the "X.509 Certificate Issuer" attribute with PyKMIP, which seems (as best I can tell) to not be supported, so when PyKMIP goes to parse the response it errors out in an unfortunate way. This manifests in the following backtrace:

```
  File "pykmip/pykmip_tests.py", line 259, in cert_test
    a = client.get_attributes(id, [attr])
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/pie/client.py", line 39, in wrapper
    return function(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/pie/client.py", line 841, in get_attributes
    result = self.proxy.get_attributes(uid, attribute_names)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/services/kmip_client.py", line 590, in get_attributes
    response = self._send_and_receive_message(request)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/services/kmip_client.py", line 1563, in _send_and_receive_message
    response.read(data)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/core/messages/messages.py", line 385, in read
    batch_item.read(istream)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/core/messages/messages.py", line 294, in read
    self.response_payload.read(tstream)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/core/messages/payloads/get_attributes.py", line 299, in read
    attribute.read(tstream)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/PyKMIP-0.8.dev0-py3.6.egg/kmip/core/objects.py", line 118, in read
    self.attribute_value.tag = Tags.ATTRIBUTE_VALUE
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'tag'
```

The following patch makes this rather more friendly by instead throwing an exception describing the problem.

```
diff --git a/kmip/core/objects.py b/kmip/core/objects.py
index 195ccb3..90dd2df 100644
--- a/kmip/core/objects.py
+++ b/kmip/core/objects.py
@@ -112,6 +112,8 @@ class Attribute(Struct):
             enum_type = name
 
         value = self.value_factory.create_attribute_value(enum_type, None)
+        if value is None:
+            raise Exception("No value type for %s" % (enum_name))
         self.attribute_value = value
         self.attribute_value.tag = Tags.ATTRIBUTE_VALUE
         self.attribute_value.read(tstream)
```

I spotted your SciPy 2015 paper submission and decided to have a look at your project - I've previously enjoyed playing around with agent based models in [Netlogo](http://ccl.northwestern.edu/netlogo/index.shtml). Mesa looks like a nice to a way of doing ABM in Python!

In your forest fire notebook, I noticed you TODO item ("Add a MatPlotLib visualization") and as the author of a new library called [HoloViews](http://ioam.github.io/holoviews/) that is designed for exploring models and animating data, I decided to give it a go. Here is [my version of your notebook](http://nbviewer.ipython.org/gist/jlstevens/9c7835e4e21c5d844ded)

Obviously you should employ whatever approach you feel most comfortable with. That said, I think you will agree that  HoloViews has managed to generate some nice output with very little code!

It seemed worth making you aware of an new, alternative approach to tackling visualization problems that avoids working with matplotlib directly.

issue_title: tests/_site/templates/layout.html omits {% block content_wrapper %}
issue_body:
I have recently been working on improving `OSCAR_HIDDEN_FEATURES` so that it supports the `"wishlists"` and `"alerts"` keys.  One facet of my effort was to hide the "Wish Lists" link on `/accounts/profile` when the `"wishlists"` feature is hidden.

My test plan for this particular modification is very simple:
1. Render `/accounts/profile` and verify that a link to `/accounts/wishlists` is present.
2. Use a context manager to temporarily add `"wishlists"` to `OSCAR_HIDDEN_FEATURES`, render `/accounts/profile`, and verify that a link to `/accounts/wishlists` is **not** present.

The following test case implements this plan:

``` python
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse

from oscar.test.testcases import WebTestCase


class TestProfilePage(WebTestCase):
    is_anonymous = False

    def test_wishlists_link_exists(self):
        account_page = self.get(reverse('customer:profile-view'))

        html = account_page.content.decode('utf-8')
        self.assertTrue(reverse('customer:wishlists-list') in html)

    def test_wishlists_link_does_not_exist(self):
        with self.settings(OSCAR_HIDDEN_FEATURES=['wishlists']):
            account_page = self.get(reverse('customer:profile-view'))

        html = account_page.content.decode('utf-8')
        self.assertFalse(reverse('customer:wishlists-list') in html)  
```

Unfortunately, `TestProfilePage.test_wishlists_link_exists` fails.

I have investigated the source of this failure and determined that it arises from the differences between `src/oscar/templates/oscar/layout.html` and `tests/_site/templates/layout.html`.  The former defines `{% block content_wrapper %}`, while the latter does not.  This is significant because `templates/oscar/customer/baseaccountpage.html` extends `templates/oscar/layout_2col.html` and renders the left navigation (where I expect the link to appear) into `{% block column_left %}`.  `{% block column_left %}` is defined by `layout_2col.html` within `{% block content_wrapper %}`.

Thus, because `tests/_site/templates/layout.html` does not define `{% block content_wrapper %}`, `{% block column_left %}` is not rendered, and `TestProfilePage.test_wishlists_link_exists` fails.

I suspect that `tests/_site/templates/layout.html` has been simplified to avoid rendering unnecessary HTML during tests.  This issue documents a case where rendering the full `layout.html` could contribute to improved test coverage.

issue_title: Feature Request: ECMAScript bindings for .NET
issue_body:
This is a continuation of my previous bug report that was placed poorly ( see: dotnet/corefx/issues/31803 ). In general this is a request about getting the old JScript .NET updated to work with more modern ECMAScript Syntax and on other platforms. In general the base ECMAScript standard has adopted some features found in C# that can be used to help with implementing a updated version of the language.

Major Implementation Problems ( Specification wise):

* The following keywords are not supported yet: interface, package, private protected, and public. These are highly useful for data management in .net. However we can use the JScript.NET syntax for now on this.
* Syntax for specifying Data types ahead of time is not set yet, but a suggestion on this has been made ( see: https://ecmascript-daily.github.io/pages/status-of-static-typing-in-ecmascript/ ). This effects the following...
  * Variable data types
  * function parameter data types.
  * function return data type.
* Classes can only have 1 super class - This is solved by using class inheritance from expressions. 

Elements helping with Implementation: 
* ECMAScript 2015 (aka ECMAScript 6th edition) ( see: https://github.com/rse/es6-features or http://es6-features.org/ for examples) - This version of ECMAScript adds most of the necessary elements for .NET language Bindings. In general the major features that can be used are as follows...
  * Block Scoping for variables and functions 
  * Lamda Opeator ( aka Arrow Functions) - This feature is symantically similar to the C# Lambda expressions.
  * Lexical This for classes and arrow functions.
  * Unicode Strings and regular expressions.
  * Constant variables
  * Default values for function paramaters.
  * Template Literals - This could pose a few problems, but in general this is extremely useful for string interpolation.
  * Generator functions - These behave a lot like the C# generator functions, but the syntax is different.
  * import/export statements ( aka Modules) - The import statements from JScript.NET do not need any changes here, but there is still some major differences that need addressing.
  * Classes - Some basic Syntax changes from JScript.NET did are required, but in general the format is a lot cleaner. The only issue is as follows... 
     * class constructors use the function "constructor" now.
     * methods no longer require function keyword in classes.
     * static member functions.
  * strings support new search methods ( "startsWith", "endsWith", and "includes".
  * Proxy handlers.
  * Typed Arrays - This is useful for static types like signed integers, unsigned integers, single precision floating point, and double precision floating point.
* ECMAScript 2016 ( aka ECMAScript 7th edition) - This mostly cleans up implementation details for ECMAScript 2015. The new features for ECMAScript 2017 are the exponentiation operator ( ** ) and Array.prototype.includes.
* ECMAScript 2017 - This adds a lot more features.
  * Shared Memory and Atomics ( see: https://github.com/tc39/ecmascript_sharedmem )
  * async Functions parameters( see: https://tc39.github.io/ecmascript-asyncawait/  )
  * String Padding ( see: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-string-pad-start-end )

Side References which can help with implementing the Feature:
* Jscript .NET Reference Pages (From Visual Studio 2010 ) - https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/visualstudio/visual-studio-2010/x85xxsf4(v%3dvs.100)
* TypeScript ( https://www.typescriptlang.org/ ) - The syntax for typed functions and variables is a clear match to what is used in JScript.NET.
* Scala.js ( https://www.scala-js.org/ ) - This also follows some of the symantics used by JScript .NET.
* JS++ by Onux Corp ( see: https://www.onux.com/jspp/ ) - This follows a more c-like approach to defining variable types.

issue_title: Bootstrapping dependencies failure on Mac OS X
issue_body:
Running the letsencrypt script, I get an error when bootstrapping dependencies

```
$ ./letsencrypt-auto --agree-dev-preview --server --debug       https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/directory auth
Bootstrapping dependencies for Mac OS X...
./letsencrypt-auto: line 36: : command not found
```

I believe this is because the ExperimentalBootstrap invocation for Mac OS X [does not require the sudo argument](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/e04c67ba8c40dee06f7a436b63b3d706ccdc1ad5/letsencrypt-auto#L95), so the command that is executed [here](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/e04c67ba8c40dee06f7a436b63b3d706ccdc1ad5/letsencrypt-auto#L36) is `"" "/bootstrap/mac.sh"`.

issue_title: [Ask About Tinode ACS]
issue_body:
Hello, Gene

I have question regarding Tinode ACS.

So I'm still not fully understand why would we need both `ModeWant` & `ModeGiven` when defining user access to the topic. Shouldn’t be `ModeGiven` only is enough to define it?😅 

I mean `ModeWant` is only useful when user want to mute the conversation, right? But for the most of the use cases, `ModeGiven` should be enough, right?

For example when group owner want to promote a member to become admin, it would give the member `"AS"` permissions. Naturally, user would become admin directly. 

But since there is a user's `ModeWant`, user need to elevate itself to admin first before it able to become the admin (set its `ModeWant` to also include `"AS"`). So why must we take this two steps when just once should be enough?

Maybe there is a design consideration behind this implementation?

Thanks

issue_title: The on_assign callback for subscribe provides an empty partitions list
issue_body:
We do custom partition/offset tracking, so we run our consumers with `enable.auto.commit=False` and `group.id=None` and we call `Consumer.subscribe` with a callback set for `on_assign`. Usually, our initial call to `subscribe` results in an empty `partitions` parameter to the `on_assign` callback. We added retry logic around our `subscribe` calls, and we generally get a valid `partitions` list in `on_assign` within a minute or so.

From this, it seems like initially the `Consumer` isn't really ready to field `subscribe` calls with `on_assign` but eventually becomes ready (perhaps related to initial metadata requests running in the background?). Is there some way for users to determine this readiness and delay calls to subscribe? Our retries are a bit hacky and I'd like to remove them.

Or, is our usage horribly wrong, requiring a new approach to our offset management?

issue_title: Default --fallback-url causing 403 error after change to restrict non HTTPS access to PyPI
issue_body:
After the changes made to PyPI which restrict access to only HTTPS (https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2017-October/031712.html), the default fallback url used by pypiserver does not work anymore as it uses HTTP.

`  --fallback-url FALLBACK_URL
    for packages not found in the local index, this URL will be used to
    redirect to (default: http://pypi.python.org/simple)`

The default for this needs to be changed to https in order to work with the new changes.


**Workaround** is to add `--fallback-url https://pypi.python.org/simple` as an option when starting pypiserver.

issue_title: Add setup_requires to pyproject.toml
issue_body: As part of [PEP 518](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0518/), build dependencies should now go into `pyproject.toml`. I think that only `pip>=10.0` supports this feature at the moment, so I don't think we can remove `setup_requires` entirely, but the two do not conflict, because `pip` will install all the setup requirements *before* invoking `setup.py`, which will thus find all its setup requirements already satisfied.

issue_title: Parser uses today's month/day when parsing date of format (DDmonYYYY) with fuzzy=True
issue_body:
```python
>>> dateutil.parser.parse('13NOV2017', fuzzy=True)
datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 13, 0, 0)  # --- today's date

>>> dateutil.parser.parse('13NOV2017', fuzzy=True, dayfirst=True)
datetime.datetime(2017, 4, 13, 0, 0)  # --- today's date
```
Without fuzzy, it raises an error.

These should parse to the proper dates. I am not sure how the tokenization is done, but a possible solution could be to use an alphabet character (in addition to what I think is used - any non-alphanum character)

issue_title: Bulk Index Should Break Up Large Requests
issue_body:
When performing a bulk index, the client builds one massive json structure and sends it off in a single request.  However, on certain platforms (e.g., Google App Engine) HTTP requests are limited in size (in app engine's case to 10MB).  When you request more than that, the request will fail.

To fix the issue on app engine, the bulk indexer should break up the requests into manageable chunks and send one request after the payload reaches a set size.  I understand that this change creates extra requests to be sent, so the chunk size should be configurable and you should be able to turn it off.

I have a fix for this in my branch, I'll issue a pull request shortly.

Thanks

issue_title: Bottle plugin path regex bug when using argument type in the url
issue_body:
When parsing the path of a bottle app in the form: `/path/to/<resourceId:int>`, the generated API path becomes `/path/to/{int}`.

reproduce:

    import re
    RE_URL = re.compile(r'<(?:[^:<>]+:)?([^<>]+)>')
    path = '/path/to/<resourceId:int>'
    RE_URL.sub(r'{\1}', path)  # --> '/path/to/{int}'

This can be fixed easily by replacing RE_URL with: `re.compile(r'<([^:]+)(?::.+)?>')`

issue_body:

foo = fields.Nested(lambda: Foo)

```

issue_title: Bug with nested schemas and custom validators
post_dump is passing a list of objects as original object
issue_body:
Hi,

I think post_dump with pass_original=True should pass the original object related to the data serialized and not a list of objects which this object belongs to.

``` python
from marshmallow import fields, post_dump, Schema

class DeviceSchema(Schema):
    id = fields.String()

    @post_dump(pass_original=True)
    def __post_dump(self, data, obj):
        print(obj)  # <-- this is a list

devices = [dict(id=1), dict(id=2)]
DeviceSchema().dump(devices, many=True)
```

In the above example, the parameter `obj` is a list of devices rather than the device object itself.

What do you think?

issue_title: Should PATCH work when PUT is not an allowed method?
issue_body:
I ran in to this behavior when adding a PATCH method to my api. The expected behavior was that including *patch* in *allowed_methods* would allow PATCH to create new resources. The actual behavior was that it would not create new resources without *put* being included.

I trailed through the code for how PATCH works and the logic for allowing PATCH is to check for *put*

[resources.py#L1543](https://github.com/django-tastypie/django-tastypie/blob/master/tastypie/resources.py#L1543) (note, I did not experiment with different combinations of allowed methods, I only experimented with adding *put*)

```python
if len(deserialized[collection_name]) and 'put' not in self._meta.detail_allowed_methods:
    raise ImmediateHttpResponse(response=http.HttpMethodNotAllowed())
```

Is this as expected? If so, maybe it would be useful to add documentation about how *allowed_methods* should be used for PATCH.

If this is not as expected, then the code for checking should have the expected logic for what happens when different combinations of methods are allowed. (also, maybe instead of the new conditions, refactor the logic to a helper, otherwise it could get hairy to read. e.g. `is_allowed` or somesuch.)

I could try making a patch, but I wanted to talk to people first to make sure it is not just a misunderstanding on my part about what is expected behavior.

issue_title: pip install djangorestframework-jsonapi fails
issue_body:
Running `pip install djangorestframework-jsonapi` fails as documented on Read the Docs. I believe the reason is that pip fails to recognize all the available version numbers appropriately.

According to the setuptools docs, the `-` in the version number makes the posted version a "post-release" number. https://pythonhosted.org/setuptools/setuptools.html#specifying-your-project-s-version

Here is the output I saw when I tried to run the command:

```bash
$ pip install djangorestframework-jsonapi
Downloading/unpacking djangorestframework-jsonapi
  Could not find a version that satisfies the requirement djangorestframework-jsonapi \
(from versions: 2.0.0-alpha.1, 2.0.0-alpha.2, 2.0.0-alpha.3, 2.0.0-alpha, 2.0.0-beta.1)
Cleaning up...
No distributions matching the version for djangorestframework-jsonapi
Storing debug log for failure in /home/matt/.pip/pip.log
```

I don't know for sure, but my guess is that in the absence of an actual version (i.e., `2.0.0`), pip gives up when it only finds post release versions.

issue_title: add param --update-if-exists to senza create to make it idempotent
issue_body:
It would be nice if we got the same result when calling `senza create` several times with the same parameters.
But at the moment, the second time `senza create` is called, we get an error 'Stack already exists`.

It would be useful to have a parameter like `--update-if-exists`.

That might required to add a function to check if the stack-name exists, and redirect to `senza update` if it returns True. 

Right now we have a bit of bash to do this:
```
stack_name=$(grep 'StackName:' "${SENZA_TEMPLATE}" | awk '{print $2}')

# check if stack exists
if [[ ! -z $(senza --region ${EC2_REGION} list --output tsv | awk '{print $1 ":" $2}' | grep ${stack_name}:${STACK_VERSION}) ]]; then
  echo "Stack '${stack_name}' exits. Updating..."
  senza_command=update
else
  echo "Stack '${stack_name}' does not exit. Creating..."
  senza_command=create
fi

# create/update stack
senza "${senza_command}" \
    --stacktrace-visible \
    --region "${REGION}" \
    --parameter-file "${SENZA_PARAM_FILE}" \
    "${SENZA_TEMPLATE}" \
    "${STACK_VERSION}" \
    dockerTage="${TAG}"
```

However this is so ugly we put it in a separate file instead of having a Makefile. And that result in having to pass all those variables(REGION, SENZA_PARAM_FILE, SENZA_TEMPLATE, STACK_VERSION, TAG) from the Makefile to the shell script, which means the script itself becomes something to maintain...

issue_title: Sort mismatch at argument #1 for function (declare-fun = (Real Real) Bool)
issue_body:
When running z3 build at commit fefd00aa4980dd7410f2315a6e3033c349f6b266 on the input file shown below, I get the error message:

```
(error "query failed: Sort mismatch at argument #1 for function (declare-fun = (Real Real) Bool) supplied sort is Int")
```

Judging by the error messages I received for other input files it seems like `duality` doesn’t support mixing reals and integers, so this is probably mostly a confusing error message. This example doesn’t use `=` anywhere. (I removed everything possible from the example so it’s pretty meaningless at this point).


```smt
(set-logic HORN)
(set-option :fixedpoint.engine duality)
(set-option :int-real-coercions false)
(declare-var HEAP (Array Int Int))
(declare-var a Int)
(declare-var i Int)
(declare-var n Int)
(declare-var s Real)
(declare-rel QUERY ())
(declare-rel Inv (Int Int Int Real (Array Int Int)))
(rule
 (=> (not (<= n 0))
     (Inv 0 a n 0.0 HEAP)))
(rule
 (=> (and (Inv i a n s HEAP) (>= i n))
     QUERY))
(rule
 (=> (Inv i a n s HEAP)
     (Inv i a n (/ (to_real (select HEAP a)) (to_real n)) HEAP)))
(query QUERY)
```

issue_title: Incorrect usage of Flask's app context during testing
issue_body:
Our `keg.testing:ContextManager` is designed to cache the application instance as well as the app context.  However, after reading [the Flask docs on app context](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/appcontext/), it has become obvious that I'm using the app context wrong.  Despite it's name, it's not intended to have the same life cycle as the app instance.  It's intended to have a lifetime almost the same as the request context.

This is actually the underlying reason behind the bug I thought Flask-WTF had: https://github.com/lepture/flask-wtf/issues/301

It looks like some kind of pytest fixture support is what we really need in order to make the app instance available to our tests instead of relying on `flask.current_app`.

issue_title: Feature proposal: Differentiable AUC approximation loss function for binary classification
issue_body:
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) metric is non-differentiable, but a differentiable approximation exists (Calders & Jaroszewicz, 2007) that is based on the AUC's interpretation as the probability that the classifier ranks a randomly chosen true positive higher than a randomly chosen false positive (H.L. van Trees, 1968).

I have implemented the function on the bottom of the second page of Calders & Jaroszewicz (2007) using Theano functions, and have verified that the output values match those of sklearn.metrics.roc_auc_score for large enough sample sizes.  Assuming I correctly follow the contribution guidelines, would a pull request with this feature be accepted?  The AUROC is a common evaluation metric for binary classification competitions on Kaggle, and I noticed some demand for it when searching the internet for an implementation.  Also, I'm not sure whether this would belong in lasagne or in theano.tensor.nnet, so I will be posting on there as well.

issue_title: Allow setting a different default `tmp_storage_class`
issue_body:
#251 allowed us to specify the class used for temporary storage of a file uploaded in the admin by setting `tmp_storage_class` on the `ModelAdmin` (or other `ImportMixin` subclass).

If you need a different subclass to solve the problem of horizontally scaled application servers, as described in #251 then you probably want to apply that change to all ImportMixins.

Therefore, we should introduce a `IMPORT_EXPORT_TMP_STORAGE_CLASS` setting that can be used to provide a global override.

issue_title: remove "uses_netloc" parameter and attribute to .__init__ and .replace
issue_body:
In twisted.python.url I tried *very* hard to eliminate the use of the confusing, vague, and antiquated term "netloc".  (NB: neither https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986 nor https://url.spec.whatwg.org includes the string "netloc").  I would therefore like to eliminate its use in `uses_netloc` as well.

`uses_netloc` describes an attribute of a scheme.  However, in its current incarnation it can be made nonsensically inconsistent with the scheme it's actually using.  Additionally, schemes also have other attributes, such as a name, and a default port number.  (Possibly more in the future?)  As such, the presence of the scheme registry makes URL objects kinda/sorta mutable after the fact.

My suggestion for a replacement would be for `URL` to reference a structured `scheme` *object*; the external scheme registry could then be a collection of such objects.  If a URL is created before the registry is populated, its scheme object could then be inconsistent with the global one, but it could be replaced.  At this point, I believe schemes would just have the three attributes given above (i.e. the parameters to `registerScheme`.

issue_title: bump minimum numpy version?
issue_body:
I noticed that the `README.md` states that we support numpy >=1.6.2, but 1.7.2 seems to be the oldest version we test on Travis.  

Is there any objection to bumping the minimum numpy version?  I would be inclined to bump to **1.9** which is almost exactly 2 years old at this point.  What do others think?

Two recent examples where we wanted to use newer functionality (there are probably more I am forgetting):
in #168 we have a substitute function for `np.full` which was only introduced in 1.8
For #172, it would be nice to have the `dtype` argument to `np.linspace` which was introduced in 1.9

issue_title: SPINEL - protocol concerns
issue_body:
some issues with the SPINEL protocol need to be addressed, specifically these deal with power management and sleepy devices.

## Issue 1 - the NCP may be in a deep sleep.

The NCP device may be asleep, or in an ultra low power state and not ready to respond quickly  (immediately) to a host that may be transmitting data.

Typically a GPIO signal from the HOST to wake, or draw the attention of the slave.

The simple approach would be to think the SPI-CS signal is the solution. The reason this fails is many SPI hosts will drop the CS to active, and immediately (within ZERO micro seconds) begin transmitting (clocking) data out of the SPI port. 

Depending upon the sleep state of the NCP and the wakup timing requirements the NCP may not be able to respond, and configure it self for the SPI transfer prior to the host transmitting (or perhaps the UART baudclock source needs to be turned back on)

Examples include:  Interrupt servicing over head at "slow (ie: 32khz) sleep clock rate", length of time to re-enable the on chip oscillator, startup the external crystal and walk the internal power regulators back up to operating voltages.

This can often be made worse when the SPI interface is operating at 10mhz, or higher (vrs the 32khz sleep clock the NCP is waking up from).

The result is the Host & NCP slave can be "off-by-a-bit" or two, or three during the transfer and this problem only occurs rarely... and nobody can figure out why or when this occurs.. maybe the chip wakes up slower when it is very cold (think sensor inside a freezer, or a pool sensor in midwinter)

What is missing is a "NCP-IS-READY" hand shake back to the host.
(total 2 pins)

## Issue 2 How fast can the HOST wake up?

The NCP may need to wake the host up and tell the host there is data to be sent.

This slightly discussed in the SPINEL protocol and repeated here for completeness.

What is not considered is this: A battery powered Linux host might be asleep and in a low power state (example: A cell-phone-data-only device connected to an NCP) the NCP cannot just "raise the request" and start transmitting out the UART... that host might also need to "wake up" and turn clocks on, enable PLLs and so forth before the UART baudrate clock is operational.

Typically another GPIO pair (ncp to HOST) is used to indicate this request.
total: 4 pins.

## Issue 3 - Timing (latency) Problems 

COST PROBLEM - every pin on a chip costs money, or makes a product require a more expensive chip with more pins to provide the solution (this is great for those selling the chip, but bad for buyer of the chip)

A fundamental truth is this:  Digital signals can cross 3 to 4 inches on a circuit board without problems at relatively high frequency. There is a myth that UARTs are not reliable, that was true when the signal ran across 300 feet of wire from one end of a factory to another end of the factory. Here we are dealing with 3 to 4 inches of signal traces on a circuit board or small flex cable. The state of the art is such that you do not have signal quality problems. The truth is often something else. The problem is often the power up sequences (above) or this race condition, or you are writing very in-efficient C code.

In general, one does not want 4 GPIO hand shake pins 

- To solve issue 1  (host -> ncp) requires 2 pins, a request and ack
- To solve issue 2 (ncp -> host) requires 2 pins, a request and ack.
- 

The economic need is this:
    2 pins UART (txd/rxd)  + 2 GPIO handshake = total 4
    3 pins SPI  (mosi,miso,clock)  + 2 GPIO handshake = total 5

Notice that the SPI does not use an actual CS signal - in theory it is present, just not hooked up or the GPIO pin is reconfigured in a "non-SPI-mode" and is used as a GPIO pin instead. 

If the slave (NCP) requires this signal, the HOST_REQACK can be used as the SPI_CS signal. The slave will see the SPI_CS assert, then a seemingly long delay before the SPI_CLK starts.

## Issue 4  - hardware design problem, power management

Sometimes the GPIO signals are also used to enable power to voltage level shifters between different power domains (ie: maybe the HOST uses VDD-IO as 1.2v, but the radio uses 3.3v?) Will enabling the level shift cost another GPIO pin? Or can we reuse one of the HANDSHAKE GPIO signals? The Money answer is simple: An existing GPIO signal needs to be reused reused.

## The Timing Race Problem that occurs because of issues 1, 2, 3 and 4.

The race condition happens because of economizing the number of GPIO signals

The race condition is this:

## At Time 0 - 
Both the Host and the NCP decide "i-need-to-send-data"
Both assert their single GPIO signal at the exact same time.
  
the host is often a linux machine, the user level IRQ latency is horrible compared to the NCP-microcontroller. Latency is defined as:  NCP-GPIO assert, to HOST-GPIO-Ready assert, or further first bit has transferred over the connection.

## The RACE is this:

The host assumes the slave responded "OK-READY" via the GPIO, however the slave is sending "REQUEST_TO_SEND"

Depending upon which packet is "longer" - the other side will need to insert "SPI-STUFFING" values.
(the UART case does not require stuffing values)

## Consider the scenario: 

- The host begins transmitting a very tiny packet.
- The slave begins transmitting a very LARGE packet.
- Note: the opposite can also occur.

## At time HOSTDATA_end

When the host finishes, in the SPI case, the host will "drop the GPIO" or stop the SPI clock
The slave however is still sending data.

## In the UART case

The host can easily tell if the target has sent anything.
do you have bytes in the UART RX buffer? yes or no.
   
IF YES - determine the length of the message.
And if required wait until the end of the message before dropping the handshake pin.

## In the SPI case

Assuming the host (linux) is a SPI-MASTER, the NCP will be the SPI-SLAVE role, the side sending the smaller packet must send "stuffing" values over the wire.

Or - maybe the (the race condition is occurring) the NCP slave is sending a response while handling an incoming message.

Because we limit to only 1 transfer per handshake..

Again, the host needs to examine the first (N) bytes of the SPI transfer and determine
Question 1:  Is the slave sending "stuffing values"
OR question 2: Is the slave sending a packet?

The host needs to examine the partial SPI transfer and determine: Was this stuffing values? or Is this the start of a message?  And if so - how many more SPI clocks are required? Or how long can we wait to turn the power off to the level-shift devices?

Recall that - we only transfer a single message per handshake, and the message will always start at an appropriate place (where the sender REQACK signal was first asserted).  We never start a message in the middle of, or during the transfer of another message. The transfer then becomes some what atomic, due to the REQACK framing that occurs.

## Summary

Only one transfer is performed per "handshake". This means the host must deassert the HOST_REQACK signal at the end of the transfer. (more about this below)

To send (HOST -> NCP)

- GPIO (1) = HOST REQACK, the host asserts this to send data.
- GPIO (2) = SLAVE REQACK, the slave indicates it is ready to receive.

To send (SLAVE -> NCP)

- GPIO (2) = SLAVE REQACK, the slave asserts this indicating it needs to send.
- GPIO (1) = HOST REQACK, the host asserts this indicating it is ready to receive

For UARTS - this can be done with the RTS/CTS signal mapping and various IO/CTL operations
But honestly using the GPIO api is a better solution.

For SPI:

- the host (linux) is always a master (some smartphone chips are really dumb, and cannot operate in slave mode) we can assume these have more resources. 
- SPI requires the SCLK, MOSI, MISO - we cannot avoid these.
- 

The SPI_CS is something we can work around. On the slave side may require the SPI_CS signal to operate correctly. If so - the HOST_REQACK signal can function as a SPI_CS signal.

While the host may generate a SPI_CS - it is not really required or usable (see issues 1 and 2 above) this signal can either be (a) left as a no-connect, or (b) the mode of the HOST_SPI_CS signal could be set to "GPIO mode" and controlled via software.

## FAST TURNAROUND - streaming packets.

Often in the NCP (micro-controller) this is not an issue, within a few microseconds it is ready to go.

The host side, from user space through the kernel and out the GPIO driver is slow. We can use this to our advantage.  

First: Measure latency. 

Write a simple user space 'pulse-the-gpio' application on Linux and measure fastest time an 
The code needs to perform a WRITE pin (X [toggling the pin]), then READ pin (Y). Measure the period of the wave form, this is 2x the fastest speed the board change a GPIO Pin.  Note: Linux (and windows) user space GPIO is rather slow. We will use this to our advantage.

Second: 

Pick 50% of that period - the NCP must meet this timing requirement: From END OF DATA to SLAVE_REQACK de-assert - must be completed in that (50%) time window.

OR - The host must insert a busy wait( as described below )

The host at the end of any transfer:

- step 1 - write the HOST_REQACK pin, 
- step 2 - if required insert a short sleep (often this is not required)
- step 3 - read the SLAVE_REQACK pin
- step 4 - determine: Loop for more data, or wait for another interrupt.
- Note: this technique is often found in UART drivers where the hardware supports a FIFO buffer, you will notice the actual hardware IRQ contains a loop - that drains the fifo. That is effectively what we are doing here, the loop draining the transfer buffers.
- 

Note that a Microcontroller based HOST is often much faster at raw GPIO io then a host.
It does vary greatly due to the architecture of the host chip ( often a 2ghz android device cannot read the GPIO pins at 2ghz... they instead require/use a bus frequency shift to a lower speed IO bus)

issue_title: Multiple decorators breaks help on subcommands
issue_body:
Make the attached modifications to [examples/subcommands.py](https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/files/1715319/subcommands.txt). I added a simple decorator, which I then added to `do_base()`. With the additional decorator, the `help` command does not behave as expected.

`help base` yields:

```
usage: nimal_decorator [-h] {foo,bar} ...

Base command help

optional arguments:
  -h, --help  show this help message and exit

subcommands:
  {foo,bar}   subcommand help
    foo       foo help
    bar       bar help
```

Notice the incorrect, and also mangled command name on the first line.

If you switch the order of the decorators on `do_base()`, i.e. so `@minimal_decorator` is first, instead of second, `help base` now behaves properly, but `help base foo` and `help base bar` are not recognized, they return the same thing as `help base`.

issue_title: Use of parse_args in cmdloop
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Bhairav Shah (Bitbucket: [bhairav](http://bitbucket.org/bhairav), GitHub: [bhairav](http://github.com/bhairav))**

---

In the cmdloop method, the following line is present:

```
#!python

(callopts, callargs) = parser.parse_args()
```

Digging deeper into optparse, parse_args() calls the below line:

```
#!python

rargs = self._get_args(args)
```

Which in turn has this:

```
#!python

    def _get_args(self, args):
        if args is None:
            return sys.argv[1:]
        else:
            return args[:]              # don't modify caller's list
```

While it may make sense for _get_args in optparse to use the sys.argv arguments, would it make sense for cmd2 to do the same?  Thinking that cmd2 doesn't need to allow reading from normal shell commandline because it is supposed to be an embedded interpreter within the program.

Wondering if cmdloop in cmd2 can be fixed in one of two ways:

1) To put an empty arg list to force _get_args to not use sys.argv:

```
#!python

(callopts, callargs) = parser.parse_args(args=[])
```

OR

2) To allow the program to put an empty list in cmdloop (though that might break compatibility with cmd:

```
#!python

cmdloop(args=[])
```

I tested with option 1, and was able to make my program work by not having it complain with the following kind of error:

<program> error: no such option: -f

Thanks.

---
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/catherinedevlin/cmd2/issue/16

issue_title: geometric_mean_score in classification_report_imbalanced is wrong?
issue_body:
Hey,

I was getting different reported values for geometric mean score when using the geometric_mean_score and was curious why. Looks like there are a few mixed up parameters in classification_report_imbalanced.

geometric_mean_score wants y_true before y_pred:
```
imblearn.metrics.geometric_mean_score(**y_true, y_pred**, labels=None, pos_label=1, average='multiclass', sample_weight=None, correction=0.0)
````

However, inside classification_report_imbalanced:
```
geo = geometric_mean_score(
            **y_pred,
            y_true,**
            labels=labels,
            average=None,
            sample_weight=None)
```

So to for index balanced geometric mean:
```
# Index balanced accuracy
    iba_gmean = make_index_balanced_accuracy(
        alpha=alpha, squared=True)(geometric_mean_score)
    iba = iba_gmean(
        **y_pred,
        y_true,**
        labels=labels,
        average=None,
        sample_weight=sample_weight)
```

On average, the results are quite different:

```
PRE     ++ REC     ++ SPE     ++ F1      ++ GEO     ++ IBA
  0.496 ##   0.485 ##   0.904 ##   0.462 ##   **0.637 ##   0.407**
```

```
                   pre       rec       spe        f1       geo       iba       sup
...
avg / total       0.50      0.48      0.90      0.46      **0.68      0.45**      2800
```


After changing param order I was able to get consistent results 

Versions:
```
